title.en: Biographies of the Marquis of Pingjin and Zhufu
title.raw: 《平津侯主父列傳》 Biographies of the Marquis of Pingjin and Zhufu

sentences.zh: 丞相公孫弘者，齊菑川國薛縣人也，字季。

translations.literal: The Chancellor Gongsun Hong was a man from Xue County in the State of Zichuan in Qi, with the courtesy name Ji.
translations.idiomatic: Chancellor Gongsun Hong hailed from Xue County in the State of Zichuan, within the lands of Qi. His courtesy name was Ji.

sentences.zh: 少時為薛獄吏，有罪，免。

translations.literal: In his youth he served as a prison clerk in Xue, committed an offense, and was dismissed.
translations.idiomatic: As a young man, he served as a prison clerk in Xue, but was dismissed after committing some offense.

sentences.zh: 家貧，牧豕海上。

translations.literal: His family was poor, and he herded swine by the sea.
translations.idiomatic: His family was destitute, and he made his living herding pigs along the seashore.

sentences.zh: 年四十餘，乃學春秋雜說。

translations.literal: At over forty years of age, he then studied the Spring and Autumn Annals and miscellaneous commentaries.
translations.idiomatic: Only after he had passed forty did he begin studying the Spring and Autumn Annals and various commentaries.

sentences.zh: 養後母孝謹。

translations.literal: He supported his stepmother with filial piety and care.
translations.idiomatic: He cared for his stepmother with devoted filial piety.

sentences.zh: 建元元年，天子初即位，招賢良文學之士。

translations.literal: In the first year of the Jianyuan era, the Son of Heaven had just ascended the throne and summoned men of virtue and learning.
translations.idiomatic: In the first year of the Jianyuan era, the newly enthroned Emperor issued a call for men of worth and scholarly talent.

sentences.zh: 是時弘年六十，徵以賢良為博士。

translations.literal: At that time Hong was sixty years old, and was summoned as a man of worth to serve as an Academician.
translations.idiomatic: Gongsun Hong was already sixty by then; he was summoned as a worthy scholar and appointed Academician.

sentences.zh: 使匈奴，還報，不合上意，上怒，以為不能，弘乃病免歸。

translations.literal: He was sent on a mission to the Xiongnu, returned and reported, but it did not accord with the Emperor's wishes. The Emperor was angered and deemed him incompetent, so Hong claimed illness and was dismissed, returning home.
translations.idiomatic: He was dispatched on a mission to the Xiongnu, but upon his return, his report displeased the Emperor. Furious, the Emperor deemed him incompetent. Gongsun Hong pleaded illness, secured his release, and went home.

sentences.zh: 元光五年，有詔徵文學，菑川國復推上公孫弘。

translations.literal: In the fifth year of the Yuanguang era, there was an edict summoning men of learning, and the State of Zichuan again recommended Gongsun Hong.
translations.idiomatic: In the fifth year of the Yuanguang era, an imperial edict went out summoning men of learning, and once again the State of Zichuan put forward Gongsun Hong's name.

sentences.zh: 弘讓謝國人曰：「臣已嘗西應命，以不能罷歸，願更推選。」

translations.literal: Hong declined and apologized to the people of the state, saying: 'I have already once gone west to answer the summons, but was dismissed for incompetence and sent home. I wish you would recommend someone else.'
translations.idiomatic: Gongsun Hong demurred, telling his countrymen: 'I once answered the summons to the capital, only to be sent home for incompetence. Please select another candidate.'

sentences.zh: 國人固推弘，弘至太常。

translations.literal: The people of the state insisted on recommending Hong, and Hong went to the Grand Master of Ceremonies.
translations.idiomatic: But the people of his state insisted on putting him forward, and so Gongsun Hong presented himself before the Grand Master of Ceremonies.

sentences.zh: 太常令所徵儒士各對策，百餘人，弘第居下。

translations.literal: The Grand Master of Ceremonies ordered all the summoned Confucian scholars to answer policy questions. Among over a hundred men, Hong's ranking was at the bottom.
translations.idiomatic: The Grand Master of Ceremonies ordered all the summoned scholars to submit policy essays. Among more than a hundred candidates, Gongsun Hong was ranked last.

sentences.zh: 策奏，天子擢弘對為第一。

translations.literal: When the essays were submitted to the throne, the Son of Heaven promoted Hong's response to first place.
translations.idiomatic: Yet when the essays reached the Emperor, the Son of Heaven himself elevated Gongsun Hong's response to first place.

sentences.zh: 召入見，狀貌甚麗，拜為博士。

translations.literal: He was summoned for an audience; his appearance was most impressive, and he was appointed Academician.
translations.idiomatic: Summoned for an audience, his bearing and appearance greatly impressed the court, and he was appointed Academician.

sentences.zh: 是時通西南夷道，置郡，巴蜀民苦之，詔使弘視之。

translations.literal: At that time roads were being opened to the southwestern barbarians and commanderies established. The people of Ba and Shu suffered from this, and an edict sent Hong to inspect the situation.
translations.idiomatic: At that time, the court was opening roads into the lands of the southwestern tribes and establishing new commanderies. The people of Ba and Shu groaned under the burden, and the Emperor dispatched Gongsun Hong to investigate.

sentences.zh: 還奏事，盛毀西南夷無所用，上不聽。

translations.literal: He returned and reported on the matter, strongly criticizing the southwestern barbarians as useless, but the Emperor did not listen.
translations.idiomatic: Upon his return, he reported vigorously that the southwestern tribes were of no use to the empire, but the Emperor would not heed his counsel.

sentences.zh: 弘為人恢奇多聞，常稱以為人主病不廣大，人臣病不儉節。

translations.literal: Hong was a man of broad and extraordinary learning. He often said that the fault of a ruler lies in not being magnanimous enough, and the fault of a minister lies in not being frugal enough.
translations.idiomatic: Gongsun Hong was a man of wide and remarkable learning. He was fond of saying that a ruler's failing lay in a lack of magnanimity, while a minister's failing lay in a lack of frugality.

sentences.zh: 弘為布被，食不重肉。

translations.literal: Hong used cloth quilts and did not eat more than one meat dish.
translations.idiomatic: Gongsun Hong slept under coarse cloth quilts and never had more than a single dish of meat at his table.

sentences.zh: 后母死，服喪三年。

translations.literal: When his stepmother died, he observed mourning for three years.
translations.idiomatic: When his stepmother passed away, he observed the full three years of mourning.

sentences.zh: 每朝會議，開陳其端，令人主自擇，不肯面折庭爭。

translations.literal: At every court deliberation, he would lay out the key points and let the ruler choose for himself, never willing to openly oppose or argue at court.
translations.idiomatic: In every court deliberation, he would lay out the essential issues and let the Emperor decide, never once confronting anyone or arguing openly before the assembled officials.

sentences.zh: 於是天子察其行敦厚，辯論有餘，習文法吏事，而又緣飾以儒術，上大說之。

translations.literal: Thus the Son of Heaven observed that his conduct was sincere and generous, his arguments more than adequate, he was versed in legal and clerical affairs, and moreover adorned these with Confucian learning. The Emperor was greatly pleased.
translations.idiomatic: The Emperor thus observed that his conduct was sincere and generous, his reasoning more than persuasive, and that he was well versed in legal and administrative matters, all of which he deftly adorned with Confucian learning. The Emperor was delighted.

sentences.zh: 二歲中，至左內史。

translations.literal: Within two years, he rose to the position of Left Prefect of the Capital.
translations.idiomatic: Within two years, he had risen to the rank of Left Prefect of the Capital.

sentences.zh: 弘奏事，有不可，不庭辯之。

translations.literal: When Hong submitted proposals and something was rejected, he did not argue about it at court.
translations.idiomatic: Whenever one of Gongsun Hong's proposals was rejected, he never argued about it at court.

sentences.zh: 嘗與主爵都尉汲黯請閒，汲黯先發之，弘推其後，天子常說，所言皆聽，以此日益親貴。

translations.literal: Once, together with the Commandant of the Palace Guard Ji An, they sought a private audience. Ji An spoke first, and Hong followed up after him. The Emperor was always pleased, and everything they said was accepted. Because of this, Hong grew daily in the Emperor's favor.
translations.idiomatic: On one occasion, he and Ji An, the Commandant of the Palace Guard, jointly sought a private audience. Ji An spoke first, and Gongsun Hong followed up with supporting arguments. The Emperor was invariably pleased and accepted everything they said. In this way, Gongsun Hong's standing grew day by day.

sentences.zh: 嘗與公卿約議，至上前，皆倍其約以順上旨。

translations.literal: He once agreed with the ministers on a joint proposal, but when they came before the Emperor, he went back on the agreement to follow the Emperor's wishes.
translations.idiomatic: There were times when he would reach an agreement with the other ministers beforehand, only to abandon the consensus once before the Emperor, aligning himself instead with the imperial will.

sentences.zh: 汲黯庭詰弘曰：「齊人多詐而無情實，始與臣等建此議，今皆倍之，不忠。」

translations.literal: Ji An confronted Hong at court and said: 'The people of Qi are full of deceit and lack sincerity. He first joined us ministers in crafting this proposal, and now he has gone back on all of it. This is disloyal.'
translations.idiomatic: Ji An confronted him before the entire court: 'Men of Qi are full of deceit and devoid of sincerity. He joined us in drafting this proposal, and now he has reversed himself entirely. This is disloyalty.'

sentences.zh: 上問弘。

translations.literal: The Emperor asked Hong about it.
translations.idiomatic: The Emperor turned to Gongsun Hong for his response.

sentences.zh: 弘謝曰：「夫知臣者以臣為忠，不知臣者以臣為不忠。」

translations.literal: Hong apologized and said: 'Those who understand your servant consider me loyal; those who do not understand me consider me disloyal.'
translations.idiomatic: Gongsun Hong bowed and replied: 'Those who truly know me regard me as loyal; those who do not know me regard me as disloyal.'

sentences.zh: 上然弘言。

translations.literal: The Emperor agreed with Hong's words.
translations.idiomatic: The Emperor accepted his answer.

sentences.zh: 左右幸臣每毀弘，上益厚遇之。

translations.literal: The favored ministers on the left and right often slandered Hong, but the Emperor treated him with ever greater favor.
translations.idiomatic: Though the imperial favorites continually maligned Gongsun Hong, the Emperor only treated him with greater generosity.

sentences.zh: 元朔三年，張歐免，以弘為御史大夫。

translations.literal: In the third year of the Yuanshuo era, Zhang Ou was dismissed, and Hong was made Imperial Secretary.
translations.idiomatic: In the third year of the Yuanshuo era, Zhang Ou was removed from office, and Gongsun Hong was appointed Imperial Secretary.

sentences.zh: 是時通西南夷，東置滄海，北筑朔方之郡。

translations.literal: At that time, routes were being opened to the southwestern barbarians, the Canghai Commandery was established in the east, and the Shuofang Commandery was being built in the north.
translations.idiomatic: At that time, the court was opening routes to the southwestern tribes, establishing the Canghai Commandery in the east, and constructing the Shuofang Commandery in the north.

sentences.zh: 弘數諫，以為罷敝中國以奉無用之地，願罷之。

translations.literal: Hong repeatedly remonstrated, arguing that it was exhausting the heartland to serve useless territories, and wished to abandon them.
translations.idiomatic: Gongsun Hong remonstrated repeatedly, arguing that these ventures were exhausting the heartland in service of worthless territories, and urged that they be abandoned.

sentences.zh: 於是天子乃使朱買臣等難弘置朔方之便。

translations.literal: Thereupon the Emperor ordered Zhu Maichen and others to challenge Hong on the advantages of establishing Shuofang.
translations.idiomatic: The Emperor then dispatched Zhu Maichen and others to debate Gongsun Hong on the merits of establishing the Shuofang Commandery.

sentences.zh: 發十策，弘不得一。

translations.literal: They raised ten arguments, and Hong could not refute a single one.
translations.idiomatic: They put forward ten arguments, and Gongsun Hong could not counter a single one.

sentences.zh: 弘乃謝曰：「山東鄙人，不知其便若是，願罷西南夷、滄海而專奉朔方。」

translations.literal: Hong then apologized and said: 'I am a rustic man from east of the mountains and did not know the advantages were so great. I wish to abandon the southwestern barbarians and Canghai and focus on supporting Shuofang.'
translations.idiomatic: Gongsun Hong conceded: 'I am but a rustic from east of the mountains and did not realize the advantages were so compelling. I would propose abandoning the southwestern tribes and Canghai to devote our resources entirely to Shuofang.'

sentences.zh: 上乃許之。

translations.literal: The Emperor then approved his proposal.
translations.idiomatic: The Emperor granted his request.

sentences.zh: 汲黯曰：「弘位在三公，奉祿甚多。

translations.literal: Ji An said: 'Hong holds a position among the Three Excellencies, and his salary is very generous.
translations.idiomatic: Ji An declared: 'Gongsun Hong holds one of the highest offices in the realm, and his salary is exceedingly generous.

sentences.zh: 然為布被，此詐也。」

translations.literal: Yet he uses cloth quilts—this is fraud.'
translations.idiomatic: Yet he sleeps under coarse cloth quilts—this is nothing but fraud.'

sentences.zh: 上問弘。

translations.literal: The Emperor asked Hong about it.
translations.idiomatic: The Emperor put the question to Gongsun Hong.

sentences.zh: 弘謝曰：「有之。

translations.literal: Hong apologized and said: 'It is so.
translations.idiomatic: Gongsun Hong bowed and replied: 'It is true.

sentences.zh: 夫九卿與臣善者無過黯，然今日庭詰弘，誠中弘之病。

translations.literal: Among the Nine Ministers, none is on better terms with me than Ji An, yet today he has confronted me at court, and truly hit upon my fault.
translations.idiomatic: Among all the Nine Ministers, none is closer to me than Ji An, yet today he has confronted me before the court and truly struck at the heart of my failing.

sentences.zh: 夫以三公為布被，誠飾詐欲以釣名。

translations.literal: For one of the Three Excellencies to use cloth quilts is indeed a deceitful affectation to fish for a good name.
translations.idiomatic: For one of the Three Excellencies to sleep under coarse cloth is indeed an affectation designed to fish for a reputation.

sentences.zh: 且臣聞管仲相齊，有三歸，侈擬於君，桓公以霸，亦上僭於君。

translations.literal: Moreover, I have heard that when Guan Zhong served as minister of Qi, he possessed the Three Gui estates, his extravagance rivaling the ruler's. Duke Huan achieved hegemony through him, yet Guan Zhong also encroached upon the ruler's prerogatives.
translations.idiomatic: Moreover, I have heard that when Guan Zhong served as chief minister of Qi, he possessed the Three Gui estates and lived in extravagance rivaling his lord's. Duke Huan achieved hegemony through him, yet Guan Zhong also overstepped the ruler's prerogatives.

sentences.zh: 晏嬰相景公，食不重肉，妾不衣絲，齊國亦治，此下比於民。

translations.literal: When Yan Ying served as minister to Duke Jing, he ate no more than one meat dish, his concubines did not wear silk, and Qi was also well governed—this was lowering himself to the level of commoners.
translations.idiomatic: When Yanzi served as minister to Duke Jing, he ate no more than a single dish of meat, and his concubines wore no silk, yet Qi was equally well governed—he had lowered himself to the level of the common people.

sentences.zh: 今臣弘位為御史大夫，而為布被，自九卿以下至於小吏，無差，誠如汲黯言。

translations.literal: Now I, Hong, hold the position of Imperial Secretary, yet I use cloth quilts—no different from the Nine Ministers down to the lowest clerks. It is truly as Ji An says.
translations.idiomatic: Now I hold the office of Imperial Secretary, yet I sleep under the same coarse cloth quilts as everyone from the Nine Ministers down to the lowliest clerk. It is exactly as Ji An says.

sentences.zh: 且無汲黯忠，陛下安得聞此言。」

translations.literal: Moreover, without Ji An's loyalty, how would Your Majesty have heard these words?'
translations.idiomatic: And yet, were it not for Ji An's loyalty, how would Your Majesty ever have heard such words?'

sentences.zh: 天子以為謙讓，愈益厚之。

translations.literal: The Emperor considered him modest and self-effacing, and treated him with ever greater favor.
translations.idiomatic: The Emperor regarded this as a display of modesty and self-effacement, and favored him all the more.

sentences.zh: 卒以弘為丞相，封平津侯。

translations.literal: In the end, Hong was made Chancellor and enfeoffed as the Marquis of Pingjin.
translations.idiomatic: In the end, Gongsun Hong was elevated to Chancellor and enfeoffed as the Marquis of Pingjin.

sentences.zh: 弘為人意忌，外寬內深。

translations.literal: Hong was by nature suspicious and jealous, outwardly tolerant but inwardly calculating.
translations.idiomatic: By nature, Gongsun Hong was suspicious and jealous—outwardly magnanimous, but inwardly scheming.

sentences.zh: 諸嘗與弘有卻者，雖詳與善，陰報其禍。

translations.literal: Those who had ever crossed Hong—even if he pretended to be on good terms—he would secretly bring misfortune upon them.
translations.idiomatic: Anyone who had ever crossed him would find that, even as Gongsun Hong feigned goodwill, he was secretly engineering their downfall.

sentences.zh: 殺主父偃，徙董仲舒於膠西，皆弘之力也。

translations.literal: The execution of Zhufu Yan and the exile of Dong Zhongshu to Jiaxi were both accomplished through Hong's efforts.
translations.idiomatic: The execution of Zhufu Yan and the banishment of Dong Zhongshu to Jiaxi were both the work of Gongsun Hong.

sentences.zh: 食一肉脫粟之飯。

translations.literal: He ate a single dish of meat with coarsely hulled millet.
translations.idiomatic: His meals consisted of a single dish of meat and coarsely hulled millet.

sentences.zh: 故人所善賓客，仰衣食，弘奉祿皆以給之，家無所餘。

translations.literal: His old friends and favored guests depended on him for clothing and food; Hong used his entire salary to provide for them, leaving nothing for his household.
translations.idiomatic: Old friends and the guests he favored all depended on him for their clothing and food. Gongsun Hong spent his entire salary supporting them, leaving nothing for his own household.

sentences.zh: 士亦以此賢之。

translations.literal: The scholars also regarded him as virtuous because of this.
translations.idiomatic: For this, the scholars of his day considered him a man of virtue.

sentences.zh: 淮南、衡山謀反，治黨與方急。

translations.literal: The Kings of Huainan and Hengshan plotted rebellion, and the investigation of their conspirators was being pursued urgently.
translations.idiomatic: When the Kings of Huainan and Hengshan plotted rebellion, the investigation of their co-conspirators was pressed with great urgency.

sentences.zh: 弘病甚，自以為無功而封，位至丞相，宜佐明主填撫國家，使人由臣子之道。

translations.literal: Hong's illness was severe. He felt that he had been enfeoffed without merit and had risen to the position of Chancellor; he should have assisted the wise ruler in pacifying the state and guiding the people along the path of loyal subjects.
translations.idiomatic: Gongsun Hong's illness grew grave. He reflected that he had been enfeoffed without merit and had risen to the rank of Chancellor; it was his duty to assist the wise sovereign in pacifying the realm and guiding the people along the path of loyal subjects.

sentences.zh: 今諸侯有畔逆之計，此皆宰相奉職不稱，恐竊病死，無以塞責。

translations.literal: Now the feudal lords harbored treasonous plans—this was all because the Chancellor had failed in his duties. He feared that should he die quietly of illness, he would have no way to fulfill his responsibilities.
translations.idiomatic: Now feudal lords had hatched treasonous plots—all because he, as Chancellor, had failed in his duties. He feared that if he died quietly of illness, he would have no way to atone for his failure.

sentences.zh: 乃上書曰：「臣聞天下之通道五，所以行之者三。

translations.literal: He then submitted a memorial saying: 'I have heard that there are five universal principles under Heaven, and three means by which to put them into practice.
translations.idiomatic: He therefore submitted a memorial: 'Your servant has heard that there are five universal principles governing all under Heaven, and three virtues by which they are put into practice.

sentences.zh: 曰君臣，父子，兄弟，夫婦，長幼之序，此五者天下之通道也。

translations.literal: They are: ruler and subject, father and son, elder and younger brother, husband and wife, and the proper order between elders and juniors. These five are the universal principles of the world.
translations.idiomatic: They are: the bond between ruler and subject, between father and son, between elder and younger brother, between husband and wife, and the proper ordering of seniors and juniors. These five constitute the universal principles of all under Heaven.

sentences.zh: 智，仁，勇，此三者天下之通德，所以行之者也。

translations.literal: Wisdom, benevolence, and courage—these three are the universal virtues of the world, the means by which the principles are put into practice.
translations.idiomatic: Wisdom, benevolence, and courage—these three are the universal virtues of all under Heaven, and the means by which the five principles are carried out.

sentences.zh: 故曰『力行近乎仁，好問近乎智，知恥近乎勇』。

translations.literal: Therefore it is said: 'To practice with vigor is close to benevolence; to love inquiry is close to wisdom; to know shame is close to courage.'
translations.idiomatic: Thus it is said: "To act with diligence approaches benevolence; to love inquiry approaches wisdom; to know shame approaches courage."

sentences.zh: 知此三者，則知所以自治；

translations.literal: He who knows these three also knows how to govern himself;
translations.idiomatic: One who understands these three virtues understands how to govern himself;

sentences.zh: 知所以自治，然後知所以治人。

translations.literal: and he who knows how to govern himself will then know how to govern others.
translations.idiomatic: and one who knows how to govern himself will then know how to govern others.

sentences.zh: 天下未有不能自治而能治人者也，此百世不易之道也。

translations.literal: There has never been anyone under Heaven who could not govern himself yet could govern others. This is an unchanging principle through a hundred generations.
translations.idiomatic: Never in all the world has there been a man unable to govern himself who could govern others. This is a principle unchanged through a hundred generations.

sentences.zh: 今陛下躬行大孝，鑒三王，建周道，兼文武，厲賢予祿，量能授官。

translations.literal: Now Your Majesty personally practices the greatest filial piety, takes the Three Kings as a mirror, establishes the Way of Zhou, combines the virtues of the civil and martial, encourages the worthy with emoluments, and assigns offices according to ability.
translations.idiomatic: Now Your Majesty personally exemplifies the highest filial piety, takes the Three Kings as his mirror, follows the Way of Zhou, unites the virtues of both the civil and the martial, rewards the worthy with emoluments, and assigns office according to ability.

sentences.zh: 今臣弘罷駑之質，無汗馬之勞，陛下過意擢臣弘卒伍之中，封為列侯，致位三公。

translations.literal: Now your servant Hong is of a worn-out and dull nature, with no meritorious military service. Yet Your Majesty has, beyond all expectation, raised me from among the common soldiers, enfeoffed me as a marquis, and elevated me to the position of one of the Three Excellencies.
translations.idiomatic: Your servant Hong is a man of worn and mediocre talent, with no battlefield merit to his name. Yet Your Majesty has, beyond all reason, raised me from the common ranks, enfeoffed me as a marquis, and elevated me to the highest echelons of government.

sentences.zh: 臣弘行能不足以稱，素有負薪之病，恐先狗馬填溝壑，終無以報德塞責。

translations.literal: Your servant Hong's conduct and ability are insufficient to match this honor. I have long suffered from the ailment of carrying firewood on my back, and I fear I will die like a dog or horse cast into a ditch, never able to repay Your Majesty's grace or fulfill my responsibilities.
translations.idiomatic: My conduct and ability fall far short of the honor bestowed upon me. I have long suffered from an ailment of the back, and I fear I shall die like a worn-out beast discarded in a ditch, never having repaid Your Majesty's grace or discharged my responsibilities.

sentences.zh: 願歸侯印，乞骸骨，避賢者路。」

translations.literal: I wish to return the marquis's seal, beg to retire with my old bones, and make way for the worthy.'
translations.idiomatic: I beg to return the seal of my marquisate and retire in my old age, clearing the path for worthier men.'

sentences.zh: 天子報曰：「古者賞有功，襃德，守成尚文，遭遇右武，未有易此者也。

translations.literal: The Emperor replied: 'In ancient times, merit was rewarded and virtue honored. In times of stability, civil virtues were esteemed; in times of crisis, martial virtues were favored. This has never changed.
translations.idiomatic: The Emperor replied: 'Since antiquity, the meritorious have been rewarded and the virtuous honored. In times of stability, the civil arts are prized; in times of crisis, martial valor is exalted. This has never changed.

sentences.zh: 朕宿昔庶幾獲承尊位，懼不能寧，惟所與共為治者，君宜知之。

translations.literal: From the time I was fortunate enough to inherit the exalted throne, I have feared I could not bring tranquility. Only through those who share in governance with me can this be achieved—this you should know.
translations.idiomatic: Ever since I was fortunate enough to inherit this exalted throne, I have lived in fear of failing to bring peace. It is only through those who share the burden of governance with me that the realm can be ordered—and this you should know well.

sentences.zh: 蓋君子善善惡惡，（君宜知之）君若謹行，常在朕躬。

translations.literal: A true gentleman approves the good and abhors the evil—you should know this. If you conduct yourself with care, you will always remain at my side.
translations.idiomatic: A true gentleman commends the good and condemns the wicked. If you continue to conduct yourself with care, you shall always remain at my side.

sentences.zh: 君不幸罹霜露之病，何恙不已，乃上書歸侯，乞骸骨，是章朕之不德也。

translations.literal: You have unfortunately contracted an illness from the frost and dew. Why does the ailment not heal? Yet you submit a memorial to return your marquisate and retire—this would only display my lack of virtue.
translations.idiomatic: You have had the misfortune of falling ill from exposure to the elements. Why has the ailment not yet healed? Yet to submit a memorial returning your marquisate and begging to retire would only proclaim my lack of virtue to the world.

sentences.zh: 今事少閒，君其省思慮，一精神，輔以醫藥。」

translations.literal: Now that affairs are somewhat less pressing, you should reduce your worries, concentrate your spirit, and supplement this with medicine.'
translations.idiomatic: Now that the press of affairs has eased somewhat, you should set aside your anxieties, gather your strength, and attend to your recovery with the help of physicians.'

sentences.zh: 因賜告牛酒雜帛。

translations.literal: He thereupon granted him leave and bestowed upon him cattle, wine, and various silks.
translations.idiomatic: The Emperor thereupon granted him leave and presented him with gifts of cattle, wine, and assorted silks.

sentences.zh: 居數月，病有瘳，視事。

translations.literal: After several months, his illness improved, and he returned to his duties.
translations.idiomatic: After several months, his condition improved and he resumed his duties.

sentences.zh: 元狩二年，弘病，竟以丞相終。

translations.literal: In the second year of the Yuanshou era, Hong fell ill and ultimately died while still serving as Chancellor.
translations.idiomatic: In the second year of the Yuanshou era, Gongsun Hong fell ill once more and died in office as Chancellor.

sentences.zh: 子度嗣為平津侯。

translations.literal: His son Du succeeded him as the Marquis of Pingjin.
translations.idiomatic: His son Du inherited the title of Marquis of Pingjin.

sentences.zh: 度為山陽太守十餘歲，坐法失侯。

translations.literal: Du served as Governor of Shanyang for over ten years, but was convicted of a legal offense and lost his marquisate.
translations.idiomatic: Du served as the Governor of Shanyang for more than ten years before being convicted of a legal offense and stripped of his marquisate.

sentences.zh: 主父偃者，齊臨菑人也。

translations.literal: Zhufu Yan was a man from Linzi in Qi.
translations.idiomatic: Zhufu Yan hailed from Linzi in the land of Qi.

sentences.zh: 學長短縱橫之術，晚乃學易、春秋、百家言。

translations.literal: He studied the arts of rhetoric and alliance-building, and only later studied the Book of Changes, the Spring and Autumn Annals, and the writings of the Hundred Schools.
translations.idiomatic: He first studied the arts of persuasion and diplomatic strategy, and only later turned to the Book of Changes, the Spring and Autumn Annals, and the writings of the Hundred Schools.

sentences.zh: 游齊諸生閒，莫能厚遇也。

translations.literal: He traveled among the scholars of Qi, but none would receive him generously.
translations.idiomatic: He traveled among the scholars of Qi, but none would offer him patronage.

sentences.zh: 齊諸儒生相與排擯，不容於齊。

translations.literal: The Confucian scholars of Qi collectively excluded and rejected him, and he was not tolerated in Qi.
translations.idiomatic: The Confucian scholars of Qi banded together to ostracize him, and he found no welcome in the land of Qi.

sentences.zh: 家貧，假貸無所得，乃北游燕、趙、中山，皆莫能厚遇，為客甚困。

translations.literal: His family was poor, and he could not obtain loans. So he traveled north to Yan, Zhao, and Zhongshan, but none would receive him generously, and as a guest he was in great hardship.
translations.idiomatic: His family was destitute, and he could obtain no loans. He traveled north to the states of Yan, Zhao, and Zhongshan, but nowhere did he find generous patronage, and his life as a wandering guest was one of great hardship.

sentences.zh: 孝武元光元年中，以為諸侯莫足游者，乃西入關見衛將軍。

translations.literal: In the middle of the first year of the Yuanguang era under Emperor Wu, he concluded that none of the feudal lords was worth seeking out, so he went west through the passes to see General Wei.
translations.idiomatic: Midway through the first year of the Yuanguang era under Emperor Wu, he concluded that no feudal lord was worth his service, and so he traveled west through the passes to seek out General Wei Qing.

sentences.zh: 衛將軍數言上，上不召。

translations.literal: General Wei spoke to the Emperor about him several times, but the Emperor did not summon him.
translations.idiomatic: General Wei recommended him to the Emperor on several occasions, but the Emperor would not summon him.

sentences.zh: 資用乏，留久，諸公賓客多厭之，乃上書闕下。

translations.literal: His funds were exhausted, and having stayed a long time, the various lords' guests had mostly grown tired of him. So he submitted a memorial at the palace gate.
translations.idiomatic: His funds ran dry, and after lingering so long, the patrons and their guests had grown weary of him. At last, he submitted a memorial directly at the palace gate.

sentences.zh: 朝奏，暮召入見。

translations.literal: He submitted his memorial in the morning and was summoned for an audience that evening.
translations.idiomatic: He submitted his memorial in the morning, and by evening the Emperor had summoned him for an audience.

sentences.zh: 所言九事，其八事為律令，一事諫伐匈奴。

translations.literal: He addressed nine matters: eight concerned laws and regulations, and one was a remonstrance against attacking the Xiongnu.
translations.idiomatic: His memorial addressed nine topics: eight concerned laws and regulations, and one was an argument against waging war on the Xiongnu.

sentences.zh: 其辭曰：

translations.literal: His words said:
translations.idiomatic: The text of his remonstrance read:

sentences.zh: 臣聞明主不惡切諫以博觀，忠臣不敢避重誅以直諫，是故事無遺策而功流萬世。

translations.literal: Your servant has heard that a wise ruler does not resent frank remonstrance, for it broadens his vision, and a loyal minister does not shrink from the threat of severe punishment to offer straight counsel. Thus no policy is overlooked and achievements endure for ten thousand generations.
translations.idiomatic: I have heard that a wise ruler does not resent blunt counsel, for it broadens his perspective, and a loyal minister does not shrink from the threat of execution to speak the truth. In this way, no policy goes unconsidered and achievements endure through the ages.

sentences.zh: 今臣不敢隱忠避死以效愚計，願陛下幸赦而少察之。

translations.literal: Now your servant does not dare conceal his loyalty or avoid death, and offers his humble plan. I hope Your Majesty will graciously pardon me and give it some consideration.
translations.idiomatic: Now I dare not conceal my loyalty or shrink from death as I present my humble counsel. I beg Your Majesty to pardon my boldness and give these words some consideration.

sentences.zh: 《司馬法》曰：「國雖大，好戰必亡；

translations.literal: The Sima Fa says: 'Though a state be great, if it loves war it will surely perish;
translations.idiomatic: The Sima Fa states: 'Though a state be great, if it delights in war it will surely perish;

sentences.zh: 天下雖平，忘戰必危。」

translations.literal: though the world be at peace, if it forgets war it will surely face danger.'
translations.idiomatic: though the world be at peace, if it forgets war it will surely court danger.'

sentences.zh: 天下既平，天子大凱，春蒐秋狝，諸侯春振旅，秋治兵，所以不忘戰也。

translations.literal: When the world was at peace and the Son of Heaven celebrated greatly, in spring he hunted and in autumn he conducted military reviews. The feudal lords in spring mustered their troops and in autumn trained them—all so as not to forget war.
translations.idiomatic: When the realm was at peace and the Son of Heaven held grand celebrations, the spring hunts and autumn military reviews continued. The feudal lords mustered their troops in spring and drilled them in autumn—all to ensure that war was never forgotten.

sentences.zh: 且夫怒者逆德也，兵者凶器也，爭者末節也。

translations.literal: Moreover, anger is contrary to virtue, weapons are instruments of ill omen, and strife is a matter of last resort.
translations.idiomatic: Moreover, wrath runs counter to virtue, weapons are instruments of ill omen, and conflict is a matter of last resort.

sentences.zh: 古之人君一怒必伏尸流血，故聖王重行之。

translations.literal: When an ancient ruler gave way to a single burst of anger, corpses would fall and blood would flow. Therefore the sage kings were cautious in resorting to it.
translations.idiomatic: Whenever an ancient ruler gave way to rage, corpses fell and blood flowed. For this reason, the sage kings were exceedingly cautious in resorting to force.

sentences.zh: 夫務戰勝窮武事者，未有不悔者也。

translations.literal: Those who pursued military victory and exhausted the affairs of war have never been without regret.
translations.idiomatic: Those who pursued military conquest and exhausted themselves in martial affairs have never escaped regret.

sentences.zh: 昔秦皇帝任戰勝之威，蠶食天下，并吞戰國，海內為一，功齊三代。

translations.literal: In the past, the First Emperor of Qin relied on the might of military conquest, consuming the world like a silkworm eating a mulberry leaf, swallowing up the Warring States, uniting all within the seas—his achievements rivaled those of the Three Dynasties.
translations.idiomatic: The First Emperor of Qin rode the momentum of military conquest, devouring the realm like a silkworm through a leaf, swallowing up the Warring States, and uniting all within the seas. His achievements rivaled those of the Three Dynasties.

sentences.zh: 務勝不休，欲攻匈奴，李斯諫曰：「不可。

translations.literal: He pursued victory without cease and wished to attack the Xiongnu. Li Si remonstrated and said: 'This must not be done.
translations.idiomatic: Yet he pursued conquest without pause and wished to attack the Xiongnu. Li Si remonstrated: 'This must not be done.

sentences.zh: 夫匈奴無城郭之居，委積之守，遷徙鳥舉，難得而制也。

translations.literal: The Xiongnu have no walled cities to dwell in, no stored provisions to guard. They move and migrate like flocking birds—they are difficult to capture and control.
translations.idiomatic: The Xiongnu have no walled cities to shelter in and no granaries to defend. They shift their camps and scatter like flocks of birds—they are nearly impossible to seize and subdue.

sentences.zh: 輕兵深入，糧食必絕；

translations.literal: If light troops penetrate deeply, their provisions will surely run out;
translations.idiomatic: Should a light force penetrate deep into their territory, its provisions will inevitably be exhausted;

sentences.zh: 踵糧以行，重不及事。

translations.literal: if supplies follow at the rear, the burden will be too great to accomplish anything.
translations.idiomatic: should supply trains follow behind, the weight of the baggage will bog down the campaign.

sentences.zh: 得其地不足以為利也，遇其民不可役而守也。

translations.literal: Gaining their land is not enough to be profitable, and encountering their people, one cannot conscript and garrison them.
translations.idiomatic: Conquering their land yields no profit, and their people can be neither conscripted nor garrisoned.

sentences.zh: 勝必殺之，非民父母也。

translations.literal: To win is to slaughter them—this is not acting as a parent to the people.
translations.idiomatic: To conquer them means slaughtering them—this is not the way of a ruler who is father and mother to his people.

sentences.zh: 靡獘中國，快心匈奴，非長策也。」

translations.literal: To exhaust and ruin the heartland merely for the satisfaction of venting anger on the Xiongnu is not a long-term strategy.'
translations.idiomatic: To exhaust the heartland for the momentary satisfaction of punishing the Xiongnu is no strategy for the long term.'

sentences.zh: 秦皇帝不聽，遂使蒙恬將兵攻胡，辟地千里，以河為境。

translations.literal: The First Emperor of Qin did not listen and dispatched Meng Tian to lead troops against the Hu, opening up a thousand li of territory with the Yellow River as the border.
translations.idiomatic: The First Emperor would not listen. He dispatched Meng Tian to lead an army against the northern tribes, carving out a thousand li of territory and establishing the Yellow River as the frontier.

sentences.zh: 地固澤（咸）鹵，不生五穀。

translations.literal: The land was nothing but marshes and salt flats, and the five grains would not grow there.
translations.idiomatic: But the land was nothing more than marshes and salt flats where the five grains could not grow.

sentences.zh: 然後發天下丁男以守北河。

translations.literal: Then he conscripted able-bodied men from across the empire to garrison the northern reaches of the Yellow River.
translations.idiomatic: He then conscripted able-bodied men from throughout the empire to garrison the northern bank of the Yellow River.

sentences.zh: 暴兵露師十有餘年，死者不可勝數，終不能踰河而北。

translations.literal: For more than ten years troops were exposed to the elements. The dead could not be counted, yet they were never able to cross north beyond the river.
translations.idiomatic: For more than ten years the troops endured harsh conditions in the open. The dead were beyond counting, yet the army could never advance north beyond the river.

sentences.zh: 是豈人眾不足，兵革不備哉？

translations.literal: Was this because there were not enough men, or because arms and armor were not adequately prepared?
translations.idiomatic: Was it for lack of manpower, or because the army's weapons and armor were inadequate?

sentences.zh: 其勢不可也。

translations.literal: The strategic situation simply did not permit it.
translations.idiomatic: The situation itself made it impossible.

sentences.zh: 又使天下蜚芻芻粟，起於黃、腄、瑯邪負海之郡，轉輸北河，率三十鐘而致一石。

translations.literal: He also ordered fodder and grain to be rushed from across the empire, from the coastal commanderies of Huang, Chui, and Langya, to be transported to the northern river. On average, thirty zhong of grain had to be shipped to deliver one dan.
translations.idiomatic: He further ordered fodder and grain to be rushed from the coastal commanderies of Huang, Chui, and Langya, transported to the northern frontier. For every thirty zhong shipped, only a single dan arrived.

sentences.zh: 男子疾耕不足於糧馕，女子紡績不足於帷幕。

translations.literal: The men toiled at the plow but could not produce enough food; the women spun and wove but could not provide enough cloth for tents.
translations.idiomatic: Men labored at the plow but could not produce enough grain to feed the army; women spun and wove but could not produce enough cloth for tents and curtains.

sentences.zh: 百姓靡敝，孤寡老弱不能相養，道路死者相望，蓋天下始畔秦也。

translations.literal: The common people were exhausted and ruined. Orphans, widows, the aged, and the weak could not support one another. The dead lined the roads as far as the eye could see. This was when the empire first turned against Qin.
translations.idiomatic: The common people were utterly exhausted. Orphans, widows, the aged, and the infirm could no longer sustain one another. The dead littered the roads as far as the eye could see. It was then that all under Heaven first turned against Qin.

sentences.zh: 及至高皇帝定天下，略地於邊，聞匈奴聚於代谷之外而欲擊之。

translations.literal: When Emperor Gaozu pacified the realm and was campaigning along the frontier, he heard that the Xiongnu had massed beyond the valley of Dai and wished to attack them.
translations.idiomatic: When Emperor Gaozu had pacified the realm and was campaigning along the frontier, he learned that the Xiongnu had massed beyond the valley of Dai and wished to strike them.

sentences.zh: 御史成進諫曰：「不可。

translations.literal: The Imperial Censor Cheng Jin remonstrated and said: 'This must not be done.
translations.idiomatic: The Imperial Censor Cheng Jin remonstrated: 'This must not be done.

sentences.zh: 夫匈奴之性，獸聚而鳥散，從之如搏影。

translations.literal: The nature of the Xiongnu is to gather like beasts and scatter like birds. Pursuing them is like trying to grasp a shadow.
translations.idiomatic: The Xiongnu gather like herds of beasts and scatter like flocks of birds. To pursue them is like trying to catch a shadow.

sentences.zh: 今以陛下盛德攻匈奴，臣竊危之。」

translations.literal: To use Your Majesty's great virtue to attack the Xiongnu—your servant privately fears the danger of this.'
translations.idiomatic: For Your Majesty to risk his great virtue by attacking the Xiongnu—I secretly tremble at the danger of it.'

sentences.zh: 高帝不聽，遂北至於代谷，果有平城之圍。

translations.literal: Emperor Gaozu did not listen and proceeded north to the valley of Dai, where he was indeed besieged at Pingcheng.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Gaozu would not listen and marched north to the valley of Dai, where he was indeed trapped in the siege of Pingcheng.

sentences.zh: 高皇帝蓋悔之甚，乃使劉敬往結和親之約，然後天下忘干戈之事。

translations.literal: Emperor Gaozu bitterly regretted it and sent Liu Jing to negotiate a marriage alliance with the Xiongnu. Only then did the empire put aside the affairs of war.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Gaozu bitterly regretted his decision and sent Liu Jing to negotiate a marriage alliance with the Xiongnu. Only then did the empire at last set aside the business of war.

sentences.zh: 故兵法曰「興師十萬，日費千金」。

translations.literal: Therefore the military treatises say: 'To raise an army of a hundred thousand costs a thousand pieces of gold per day.'
translations.idiomatic: As the military classics say: "To mobilize an army of a hundred thousand costs a thousand in gold each day."

sentences.zh: 夫秦常積眾暴兵數十萬人，雖有覆軍殺將系虜單于之功，亦適足以結怨深讎，不足以償天下之費。

translations.literal: Qin regularly amassed hundreds of thousands of troops and exposed them to hardship. Even if they achieved the feat of destroying armies, killing generals, and capturing the Chanyu, it would only be enough to deepen enmity and grudges, not enough to repay the cost to the empire.
translations.idiomatic: Qin regularly deployed hundreds of thousands of troops under the harshest conditions. Even had they destroyed armies, slain enemy generals, and captured the Chanyu himself, it would only have deepened the enmity and blood feuds—it could never have repaid the cost to the empire.

sentences.zh: 夫上虛府庫，下敝百姓，甘心於外國，非完事也。

translations.literal: To empty the state treasury above and exhaust the common people below, all to indulge one's heart against foreign nations—this is not a sound course of action.
translations.idiomatic: To drain the treasury above and exhaust the common people below, all for the satisfaction of punishing foreign nations—this is no sound course of action.

sentences.zh: 夫匈奴難得而制，非一世也。

translations.literal: The Xiongnu have been difficult to capture and control—this has not been the case for just one generation.
translations.idiomatic: The Xiongnu have been impossible to subdue—and not just for a single generation.

sentences.zh: 行盜侵驅，所以為業也，天性固然。

translations.literal: Raiding, invading, and plundering—this is their livelihood, their inborn nature.
translations.idiomatic: Raiding, invading, and plundering are their livelihood—it is their very nature.

sentences.zh: 上及虞夏殷周，固弗程督，禽獸畜之，不屬為人。

translations.literal: Going back to the reigns of Yu, Xia, Yin, and Zhou, they were never regulated or supervised but were treated as birds and beasts, not counted among civilized peoples.
translations.idiomatic: Going back to the reigns of Yu, Xia, Yin, and Zhou, the Xiongnu were never governed or supervised; they were treated as wild animals, not reckoned among civilized peoples.

sentences.zh: 夫上不觀虞夏殷周之統，而下（修）［循］近世之失，此臣之所大憂，百姓之所疾苦也。

translations.literal: To fail to look up to the traditions of Yu, Xia, Yin, and Zhou, and instead to follow the mistakes of recent ages—this is what your servant greatly fears, and what causes the people the greatest suffering.
translations.idiomatic: To ignore the precedents of Yu, Xia, Yin, and Zhou and instead repeat the mistakes of more recent times—this is what I fear most deeply, and what inflicts the greatest suffering upon the people.

sentences.zh: 且夫兵久則變生，事苦則慮易。

translations.literal: Moreover, when military campaigns drag on, turmoil arises; when hardships increase, loyalties shift.
translations.idiomatic: Moreover, when war drags on, upheaval follows; when hardship deepens, loyalties waver.

sentences.zh: 乃使邊境之民獘靡愁苦而有離心，將吏相疑而外市，故尉佗、章邯得以成其私也。

translations.literal: This causes the border people to become exhausted, miserable, and disaffected, while generals and officials suspect one another and seek advantage abroad. Thus Commandant Tuo and Zhang Han were able to pursue their private ambitions.
translations.idiomatic: The border people are driven to exhaustion and despair, breeding disaffection, while generals and officials suspect one another and seek advantage for themselves abroad. It was precisely this that allowed Commandant Tuo and Zhang Han to carve out their own domains.

sentences.zh: 夫秦政之所以不行者，權分乎二子，此得失之效也。

translations.literal: The reason Qin's governance failed was that power was divided between these two men. This is the lesson of success and failure.
translations.idiomatic: The reason Qin's authority collapsed was that power had been divided between these two men. This is the very lesson of success and failure.

sentences.zh: 故《周書》曰「安危在出令，存亡在所用」。

translations.literal: Therefore the Book of Zhou says: 'Safety and peril depend on the orders issued; survival and destruction depend on the men employed.'
translations.idiomatic: Thus the Book of Zhou declares: "Safety and peril depend upon the orders a ruler issues; survival and ruin depend upon the men he employs."

sentences.zh: 願陛下詳察之，少加意而熟慮焉。

translations.literal: I hope Your Majesty will examine this carefully, pay it some attention, and consider it thoroughly.
translations.idiomatic: I beg Your Majesty to examine this matter with care, to give it due attention, and to deliberate thoroughly.

sentences.zh: 是時趙人徐樂、齊人嚴安俱上書言世務，各一事。

translations.literal: At that time, Xu Yue of Zhao and Yan An of Qi both submitted memorials addressing the affairs of the age, each on one topic.
translations.idiomatic: At that time, Xu Yue of Zhao and Yan An of Qi each submitted a memorial addressing the affairs of the day, each on a single topic.

sentences.zh: 徐樂曰：

translations.literal: Xu Yue said:
translations.idiomatic: Xu Yue wrote:

sentences.zh: 臣聞天下之患在於土崩，不在於瓦解，古今一也。

translations.literal: Your servant has heard that the peril of the empire lies in 'earth collapse,' not in 'tile disintegration'—this has been the same from ancient times to the present.
translations.idiomatic: I have heard that the true peril of an empire lies in its foundation crumbling from below, not in its tiles breaking apart from above—this has been true since antiquity.

sentences.zh: 何謂土崩？

translations.literal: What is meant by 'earth collapse'?
translations.idiomatic: What does it mean for the foundation to crumble?

sentences.zh: 秦之末世是也。

translations.literal: The last days of Qin exemplify this.
translations.idiomatic: The final years of Qin are the very example.

sentences.zh: 陳涉無千乘之尊，尺土之地，身非王公大人名族之后，無鄉曲之譽，非有孔、墨、曾子之賢，陶朱、猗頓之富也，然起窮巷，奮棘矜，偏袒大呼而天下從風，此其故何也？

translations.literal: Chen She had neither the dignity of a lord of a thousand chariots nor a foot of territory. He was not the descendant of kings, dukes, or great men of renowned lineage. He had no reputation in his home village, nor did he possess the wisdom of Confucius, Mozi, or Zengzi, nor the wealth of Tao Zhu or Yi Dun. Yet he rose from a wretched alley, wielded a thorny staff, bared his arm, raised a great shout, and the whole empire followed him like wind. What was the reason for this?
translations.idiomatic: Chen She commanded neither the authority of a lord of a thousand chariots nor a single foot of territory. He was no descendant of kings or dukes or men of noble lineage. He had no reputation in his home village, nor the wisdom of Confucius, Mozi, or Master Zeng, nor the wealth of Tao Zhu or Yi Dun. Yet he rose from a wretched alleyway, seized a thorny staff, bared his shoulder, let out a great shout, and the entire empire followed him like wind bending the grass. What was the reason?

sentences.zh: 由民困而主不恤，下怨而上不知（也），俗已亂而政不修，此三者陳涉之所以為資也。

translations.literal: It was because the people were in distress and the ruler showed no concern; the people below harbored resentment and those above did not know it; customs had already become disordered and governance was not reformed. These three things were what Chen She relied upon.
translations.idiomatic: It was because the people were in distress and their ruler showed no concern; resentment festered below while those above remained ignorant of it; customs had fallen into disorder and the government did nothing to set them right. These three conditions were the capital upon which Chen She drew.

sentences.zh: 是之謂土崩。

translations.literal: This is what is called 'earth collapse.'
translations.idiomatic: This is what it means for the foundation to crumble.

sentences.zh: 故曰天下之患在於土崩。

translations.literal: Therefore it is said that the peril of the empire lies in 'earth collapse.'
translations.idiomatic: Therefore I say that the true peril of an empire lies in its foundation crumbling.

sentences.zh: 何謂瓦解？

translations.literal: What is meant by 'tile disintegration'?
translations.idiomatic: And what does it mean for the tiles to break apart?

sentences.zh: 吳、楚、齊、趙之兵是也。

translations.literal: The armies of Wu, Chu, Qi, and Zhao exemplify this.
translations.idiomatic: The rebellion of Wu, Chu, Qi, and Zhao is the example.

sentences.zh: 七國謀為大逆，號皆稱萬乘之君，帶甲數十萬，威足以嚴其境內，財足以勸其士民，然不能西攘尺寸之地而身為禽於中原者，此其故何也？

translations.literal: The Seven Kingdoms plotted the greatest treason. Each styled himself a lord of ten thousand chariots, with hundreds of thousands of armored soldiers. Their might was sufficient to overawe their own territories, their wealth sufficient to rally their people. Yet they could not advance westward a single inch and were themselves captured in the heartland. What was the reason?
translations.idiomatic: The Seven Kingdoms plotted the gravest treason. Each styled himself a lord of ten thousand chariots, commanding hundreds of thousands of armored soldiers. Their might was sufficient to overawe their own domains; their wealth was sufficient to rally their people. Yet they could not seize a single inch of territory to the west and were themselves captured in the heartland. What was the reason?

sentences.zh: 非權輕於匹夫而兵弱於陳涉也，當是之時，先帝之德澤未衰而安土樂俗之民眾，故諸侯無境外之助。

translations.literal: It was not that their authority was less than a commoner's or their armies weaker than Chen She's. At that time, the grace and beneficence of the former emperors had not yet declined, and the people who were content with their land and happy with their customs were many. Therefore the feudal lords had no support from beyond their own borders.
translations.idiomatic: It was not that their authority was less than a commoner's or their forces weaker than Chen She's. At that time, the grace and beneficence of the former emperors had not yet faded, and the great mass of the people remained content with their land and customs. The rebellious lords therefore found no allies beyond their own borders.

sentences.zh: 此之謂瓦解，故曰天下之患不在瓦解。

translations.literal: This is what is called 'tile disintegration.' Therefore it is said that the peril of the empire does not lie in 'tile disintegration.'
translations.idiomatic: This is what it means for the tiles to break apart. And that is why I say that the true peril of an empire does not lie in tiles breaking apart.

sentences.zh: 由是觀之，天下誠有土崩之勢，雖布衣窮處之士或首惡而危海內，陳涉是也。

translations.literal: Viewed from this perspective, when the empire truly faces a situation of 'earth collapse,' even a commoner living in a remote corner may rise as the chief instigator and endanger everything within the seas—Chen She is the proof.
translations.idiomatic: Seen from this perspective, when the empire truly faces a crumbling foundation, even a commoner from the most obscure corner can rise as the chief instigator and imperil the entire realm—Chen She is the proof.

sentences.zh: 況三晉之君或存乎！

translations.literal: How much more so if any of the lords of the Three Jin states still existed!
translations.idiomatic: How much more dangerous would it be if the lords of the old Three Jin states still survived!

sentences.zh: 天下雖未有大治也，誠能無土崩之勢，雖有彊國勁兵不得旋踵而身為禽矣，吳、楚、齊、趙是也。

translations.literal: Even if the empire has not yet achieved perfect governance, so long as there is no situation of 'earth collapse,' even powerful states with strong armies cannot turn on their heels before they themselves are captured—the cases of Wu, Chu, Qi, and Zhao prove this.
translations.idiomatic: Even if the empire has not yet achieved perfect governance, so long as its foundations remain intact, even powerful states with formidable armies cannot so much as turn around before they are themselves captured—the fates of Wu, Chu, Qi, and Zhao prove this.

sentences.zh: 況群臣百姓能為亂乎哉！

translations.literal: How much less could the assembled ministers and common people create disorder!
translations.idiomatic: Still less could the ministers and common people ever create disorder!

sentences.zh: 此二體者，安危之明要也，賢主所留意而深察也。

translations.literal: These two paradigms are the clear essentials of safety and peril, what a wise ruler should attend to and examine deeply.
translations.idiomatic: These two paradigms are the clearest keys to understanding safety and peril—the very things a wise ruler must attend to and examine with the greatest care.

sentences.zh: 閒者關東五穀不登，年歲未復，民多窮困，重之以邊境之事，推數循理而觀之，則民且有不安其處者矣。

translations.literal: Recently, the five grains have failed east of the passes, the harvests have not recovered, and many people are impoverished. On top of this, the border campaigns continue. If one follows the numbers and examines the logic, there will be people who can no longer rest easy where they are.
translations.idiomatic: In recent years, the five grains have failed east of the passes and the harvests have not recovered. The people are sinking into poverty, burdened further by the ongoing border campaigns. If one traces the pattern and follows the logic, there will soon be people who can no longer remain content where they are.

sentences.zh: 不安故易動。

translations.literal: Those who are not content are easily stirred to action.
translations.idiomatic: Those who are not content are easily stirred to revolt.

sentences.zh: 易動者，土崩之勢也。

translations.literal: Those who are easily stirred—this is the situation of 'earth collapse.'
translations.idiomatic: When the people are easily stirred, the conditions for the foundation to crumble are already in place.

sentences.zh: 故賢主獨觀萬化之原，明於安危之機，修之廟堂之上，而銷未形之患。

translations.literal: Therefore a wise ruler alone discerns the origins of all transformations, understands the critical moment of safety and danger, addresses matters from the court above, and eliminates troubles before they take shape.
translations.idiomatic: A wise ruler therefore discerns the origins of all change, grasps the pivotal moment between safety and peril, addresses the matter from the court, and eliminates threats before they take shape.

sentences.zh: 其要，期使天下無土崩之勢而已矣。

translations.literal: The essence is simply to ensure that the empire does not face a situation of 'earth collapse.'
translations.idiomatic: The essence of his task is simply to ensure that the empire's foundations never crumble.

sentences.zh: 故雖有彊國勁兵，陛下逐走獸，射蜚鳥，弘游燕之囿，淫縱恣之觀，極馳騁之樂，自若也。

translations.literal: Thus, even if there were powerful states with strong armies, Your Majesty could pursue running beasts, shoot at flying birds, range through the grand parks of leisure, indulge in the most extravagant pleasures, and enjoy the full thrill of riding—all without concern.
translations.idiomatic: Thus, even were there powerful states with formidable armies, Your Majesty could chase running game, shoot at soaring birds, roam the grand parks at leisure, indulge in the most extravagant pleasures, and revel in the thrill of the hunt—all without a care.

sentences.zh: 金石絲竹之聲不絕於耳，帷帳之私俳優侏儒之笑不乏於前，而天下無宿憂。

translations.literal: The sounds of bells, stones, strings, and flutes would never cease in your ears; the private delights behind the curtains and the laughter of jesters and dwarfs would never be lacking before you—and the empire would have no lingering worries.
translations.idiomatic: The music of bells, chimes, strings, and flutes would never leave your ears; the pleasures of the bedchamber and the laughter of jesters and entertainers would never be lacking—and the empire would have no lingering worries.

sentences.zh: 名何必湯武，俗何必成康！

translations.literal: Why must your fame match Tang and Wu? Why must your customs match those of Cheng and Kang?
translations.idiomatic: Why would your fame need to match that of Kings Tang and Wu? Why would your customs need to rival those of the reigns of Cheng and Kang?

sentences.zh: 雖然，臣竊以為陛下天然之聖，寬仁之資，而誠以天下為務，則湯武之名不難侔，而成康之俗可復興也。

translations.literal: Nevertheless, your servant privately believes that with Your Majesty's natural sagacity and your generous and benevolent nature, if you truly take the empire as your foremost concern, it would not be difficult to match the fame of Tang and Wu, and the customs of the reigns of Cheng and Kang could be revived.
translations.idiomatic: Even so, I privately believe that with Your Majesty's natural sagacity and generous nature, if you truly take the welfare of the realm as your foremost concern, then the fame of Tang and Wu would not be hard to match, and the prosperous customs of the Cheng and Kang reigns could yet be revived.

sentences.zh: 此二體者立，然後處尊安之實，揚名廣譽於當世，親天下而服四夷，餘恩遺德為數世隆，南面負扆攝袂而揖王公，此陛下之所服也。

translations.literal: Once these two paradigms are established, Your Majesty will enjoy the substance of a secure and exalted position, spread fame and glory throughout the age, win the devotion of the empire and the submission of the four barbarian peoples. The surplus of your benevolence and virtue will endure for generations. Facing south with your back to the screen, adjusting your sleeves and saluting the lords and dukes—this is what Your Majesty is worthy of.
translations.idiomatic: Once these two paradigms are established, Your Majesty will enjoy the substance of a secure and exalted throne, spread renown and glory across the age, win the devotion of all under Heaven and the submission of the four barbarian peoples. The blessings of your benevolence and virtue will endure through the generations. Facing south with your back to the imperial screen, you will graciously receive the salutations of lords and dukes—this is what Your Majesty truly deserves.

sentences.zh: 臣聞圖王不成，其敝足以安。

translations.literal: Your servant has heard that even if the plan for true kingship does not fully succeed, its lesser effects are still sufficient to bring stability.
translations.idiomatic: I have heard that even if the pursuit of true kingship does not fully succeed, its lesser effects are still sufficient to bring stability.

sentences.zh: 安則陛下何求而不得，何為而不成，何征而不服乎哉！

translations.literal: With stability, what could Your Majesty seek and not obtain? What could you attempt and not achieve? What expedition could you launch and not see submission?
translations.idiomatic: With stability, what could Your Majesty seek and not obtain? What could you undertake and not accomplish? What campaign could you wage that would not end in submission?

sentences.zh: 嚴安上書曰：

translations.literal: Yan An submitted a memorial that said:
translations.idiomatic: Yan An submitted a memorial that read:

sentences.zh: 臣聞周有天下，其治三百餘歲，成康其隆也，刑錯四十餘年而不用。

translations.literal: Your servant has heard that the Zhou possessed the empire and governed for over three hundred years. The reigns of Cheng and Kang were its zenith, when punishments were set aside and not used for more than forty years.
translations.idiomatic: I have heard that the Zhou dynasty held the empire and governed well for over three hundred years. The reigns of Kings Cheng and Kang marked its zenith—for more than forty years, punishments were set aside and never needed.

sentences.zh: 及其衰也，亦三百餘歲，故五伯更起。

translations.literal: When it declined, that too lasted over three hundred years, and so the Five Hegemons rose one after another.
translations.idiomatic: Its decline likewise lasted over three hundred years, during which the Five Hegemons rose in succession.

sentences.zh: 五伯者，常佐天子興利除害，誅暴禁邪，匡正海內，以尊天子。

translations.literal: The Five Hegemons regularly assisted the Son of Heaven in promoting benefit and removing harm, punishing the violent, suppressing wickedness, rectifying all within the seas, and thereby honoring the Son of Heaven.
translations.idiomatic: The Five Hegemons assisted the Son of Heaven by advancing the public good and eliminating harm, punishing tyrants, suppressing the wicked, restoring order to the realm, and thereby upholding the dignity of the throne.

sentences.zh: 五伯既沒，賢聖莫續，天子孤弱，號令不行。

translations.literal: After the Five Hegemons passed away, no worthy sage continued their work. The Son of Heaven was isolated and weak, and his decrees went unenforced.
translations.idiomatic: After the Five Hegemons passed from the scene, no worthy successor carried on their work. The Son of Heaven was left isolated and weak, and his decrees went unenforced.

sentences.zh: 諸侯恣行，彊陵弱，眾暴寡，田常篡齊，六卿分晉，并為戰國，此民之始苦也。

translations.literal: The feudal lords acted as they pleased: the strong oppressed the weak, the many tyrannized the few. Tian Chang usurped the throne of Qi, and the Six Ministers divided Jin among themselves, merging into the Warring States. This was when the people's suffering began.
translations.idiomatic: The feudal lords acted without restraint: the strong trampled the weak, and the many tyrannized the few. Tian Chang usurped the throne of Qi, and the Six Ministers carved Jin among themselves, ushering in the era of the Warring States. This was when the suffering of the people truly began.

sentences.zh: 於是彊國務攻，弱國備守，合從連橫，馳車擊轂，介胄生蟣蝨，民無所告愬。

translations.literal: The strong states pursued attack, the weak states prepared their defenses. Vertical and horizontal alliances were formed, chariots raced and hub collided with hub. Lice bred in the soldiers' armor, and the people had nowhere to bring their grievances.
translations.idiomatic: The powerful states devoted themselves to aggression, while the weak braced for defense. Vertical and horizontal alliances were forged, war chariots raced until their hubs clashed. Lice bred in the soldiers' armor and helmets, and the people had no one to whom they could bring their grievances.

sentences.zh: 及至秦王，蠶食天下，并吞戰國，稱號曰皇帝，主海內之政，壞諸侯之城，銷其兵，鑄以為鐘虡，示不復用。

translations.literal: Then came the King of Qin, who consumed the realm like a silkworm eating a leaf, swallowed up the Warring States, took the title of Emperor, took charge of governance within the seas, destroyed the walls of the feudal lords, melted down their weapons, and cast them into bells and bell stands to show they would never be used again.
translations.idiomatic: Then came the King of Qin, who devoured the realm like a silkworm through a leaf, swallowed up the Warring States, assumed the title of Emperor, and took command of all governance within the seas. He tore down the walls of the feudal lords, melted their weapons, and cast them into bells and bell stands as a sign they would never be needed again.

sentences.zh: 元元黎民得免於戰國，逢明天子，人人自以為更生。

translations.literal: The common people were freed from the Warring States era and encountered an illustrious Son of Heaven. Everyone believed they had been given a new life.
translations.idiomatic: The common people were delivered from the chaos of the Warring States and met what they believed to be an illustrious ruler. Every person felt as though they had been granted a new life.

sentences.zh: 向使秦緩其刑罰，薄賦斂，省繇役，貴仁義，賤權利，上篤厚，下智巧，變風易俗，化於海內，則世世必安矣。

translations.literal: If Qin had relaxed its punishments, lightened taxes, reduced corvée labor, valued benevolence and righteousness, disdained scheming for profit, honored sincerity above and discouraged cunning below, transformed customs throughout the realm—then peace would have endured generation after generation.
translations.idiomatic: Had Qin relaxed its punishments, lightened the burden of taxation, reduced the demands of corvée labor, prized benevolence and righteousness over cunning and profit, honored sincerity in those above and discouraged deviousness in those below, and transformed the customs of the entire realm—peace would surely have endured for generations.

sentences.zh: 秦不行是風而修其故俗，為智巧權利者進，篤厚忠信者退；

translations.literal: But Qin did not adopt this course and instead maintained its old customs. Those who were clever and pursued power advanced, while those who were sincere and loyal were pushed aside;
translations.idiomatic: But Qin refused to adopt this course and clung to its old ways. The cunning and the power-hungry were advanced, while the sincere and the loyal were pushed aside;

sentences.zh: 法嚴政峻，諂諛者眾，日聞其美，意廣心軼。

translations.literal: the laws were harsh and governance was severe, flatterers multiplied, and the ruler daily heard only praise. His ambitions swelled and his heart grew reckless.
translations.idiomatic: the laws grew harsher, governance more severe, and flatterers multiplied. The ruler heard only praise from morning to night, and his ambitions swelled beyond all bounds.

sentences.zh: 欲肆威海外，乃使蒙恬將兵以北攻胡，辟地進境，戍於北河，蜚芻芻粟以隨其后。

translations.literal: Wishing to extend his might beyond the seas, he dispatched Meng Tian to lead troops northward against the Hu, opening territory, advancing the frontier, and garrisoning the northern river, with fodder and grain rushed to follow behind.
translations.idiomatic: Wishing to project his might beyond the seas, he dispatched Meng Tian to lead an army northward against the Hu, carving out territory, pushing the frontier forward, and establishing garrisons along the northern river, with fodder and grain rushed in their wake.

sentences.zh: 又使尉[佗]屠睢將樓船之士南攻百越，使監祿鑿渠運糧，深入越，越人遁逃。

translations.literal: He also dispatched Commandant Tu Sui to lead the marines south to attack the Hundred Yue, and sent the Superintendent Lu to dig canals and transport grain. They penetrated deep into Yue territory, but the Yue people fled.
translations.idiomatic: He also dispatched Commandant Tu Sui to lead the naval forces south against the Hundred Yue, and sent Superintendent Lu to dig canals for transporting grain. They penetrated deep into Yue territory, but the Yue people fled into the wilderness.

sentences.zh: 曠日持久，糧食絕乏，越人擊之，秦兵大敗。

translations.literal: The campaign dragged on, provisions ran out, the Yue people attacked, and the Qin forces were decisively defeated.
translations.idiomatic: The campaign dragged on interminably, provisions ran dry, and the Yue people struck back, inflicting a devastating defeat on the Qin army.

sentences.zh: 秦乃使尉佗將卒以戍越。

translations.literal: Qin then sent Commandant Tuo to lead soldiers to garrison the Yue territory.
translations.idiomatic: Qin then sent Commandant Tuo to lead a garrison force into the Yue lands.

sentences.zh: 當是時，秦禍北構於胡，南掛於越，宿兵無用之地，進而不得退。

translations.literal: At that time, Qin's troubles were building in the north against the Hu and entangled in the south with the Yue. Troops were stationed in useless lands, advancing but unable to retreat.
translations.idiomatic: At that time, Qin's troubles mounted: in the north against the Hu, in the south entangled with the Yue. Troops were garrisoned in worthless lands—they could advance but never withdraw.

sentences.zh: 行十餘年，丁男被甲，丁女轉輸，苦不聊生，自經於道樹，死者相望。

translations.literal: This went on for over ten years. Able-bodied men donned armor, able-bodied women hauled supplies. The suffering was beyond endurance—people hanged themselves from roadside trees, and the dead lined the roads as far as the eye could see.
translations.idiomatic: This went on for more than ten years. Able-bodied men donned armor while able-bodied women hauled the supply wagons. The suffering was unbearable—people hanged themselves from trees along the roadsides, and the dead stretched as far as the eye could see.

sentences.zh: 及秦皇帝崩，天下大叛。

translations.literal: When the First Emperor of Qin died, the whole empire rose in revolt.
translations.idiomatic: When the First Emperor died, the entire empire erupted in revolt.

sentences.zh: 陳勝、吳廣舉陳，武臣、張耳舉趙，項梁舉吳，田儋舉齊，景駒舉郢，周市舉魏，韓廣舉燕，窮山通谷豪士并起，不可勝載也。

translations.literal: Chen Sheng and Wu Guang raised Chen, Wu Chen and Zhang Er raised Zhao, Xiang Liang raised Wu, Tian Dan raised Qi, Jing Ju raised Ying, Zhou Shi raised Wei, and Han Guang raised Yan. Heroes from remote mountains and hidden valleys rose together—too many to record.
translations.idiomatic: Chen Sheng and Wu Guang raised the banner in Chen; Wu Chen and Zhang Er in Zhao; Xiang Liang in Wu; Tian Dan in Qi; Jing Ju in Ying; Zhou Shi in Wei; Han Guang in Yan. From every remote mountain and hidden valley, men of ambition rose up simultaneously—too many to record.

sentences.zh: 然皆非公侯之后，非長官之吏也。

translations.literal: Yet none of them were descendants of dukes or marquises, nor were they senior officials.
translations.idiomatic: Yet not one of them was the descendant of a duke or marquis, nor the holder of any senior office.

sentences.zh: 無尺寸之勢，起閭巷，杖棘矜，應時而皆動，不謀而俱起，不約而同會，壤長地進，至于霸王，時教使然也。

translations.literal: Without a foot of power, they rose from back alleys, wielding thorny staffs. They responded to the moment and all moved together, rising without conspiracy, meeting without agreement, expanding their territory step by step until they achieved hegemony. The times had shaped them so.
translations.idiomatic: Without a scrap of authority, they rose from the back alleys, armed with nothing but thorny staffs. They responded to the times, moving as one—rising without conspiracy, converging without agreement, expanding their territory step by step until they achieved hegemony and even kingship. The times themselves had driven them to it.

sentences.zh: 秦貴為天子，富有天下，滅世絕祀者，窮兵之禍也。

translations.literal: Qin was exalted as the Son of Heaven and possessed the wealth of the entire realm, yet its line was extinguished and its sacrifices cut off—this was the calamity of exhausting its military.
translations.idiomatic: Qin held the most exalted title under Heaven and possessed the wealth of the entire realm, yet its dynasty was extinguished and its sacrifices cut off—all because of the catastrophe of endless military campaigns.

sentences.zh: 故周失之弱，秦失之彊，不變之患也。

translations.literal: Thus Zhou failed through weakness and Qin through excess of strength—both suffered from the malady of not adapting.
translations.idiomatic: Zhou fell through weakness, Qin through an excess of force—both suffered the same malady of failing to adapt.

sentences.zh: 今欲招南夷，朝夜郎，降羌僰，略濊州，建城邑，深入匈奴，燔其蘢城，議者美之。

translations.literal: Now the court desires to summon the southern tribes, receive the submission of Yelang, subjugate the Qiang and Bo, seize Huizhou, build cities, penetrate deep into Xiongnu territory, and burn their encampments. Those who advocate this call it a fine plan.
translations.idiomatic: Now the court wishes to summon the southern tribes, bring Yelang to submission, subjugate the Qiang and Bo, seize the territory of the Hui, establish cities, penetrate deep into Xiongnu territory, and burn their encampments. Those who advocate this call it a fine plan.

sentences.zh: 此人臣之利也，非天下之長策也。

translations.literal: This serves the interests of individual ministers, but is not a long-term strategy for the empire.
translations.idiomatic: This may serve the interests of individual ministers, but it is no strategy for the long-term welfare of the empire.

sentences.zh: 今中國無狗吠之驚，而外累於遠方之備，靡敝國家，非所以子民也。

translations.literal: At present, the heartland has no alarms—not even a dog's bark—yet externally it is burdened by preparations against distant regions, exhausting the state. This is not the way to nurture the people as a father nurtures his children.
translations.idiomatic: At present, the heartland is so tranquil there is not even a dog's bark of alarm, yet the state exhausts itself in preparations against distant frontiers. This is no way for a ruler to care for his people as a father cares for his children.

sentences.zh: 行無窮之欲，甘心快意，結怨於匈奴，非所以安邊也。

translations.literal: To pursue boundless ambitions, indulging one's desires and feelings, and incur the enmity of the Xiongnu—this is not the way to secure the frontier.
translations.idiomatic: To pursue boundless ambitions for the sake of momentary gratification and thereby provoke the enmity of the Xiongnu—this is no way to secure the frontier.

sentences.zh: 禍結而不解，兵休而復起，近者愁苦，遠者驚駭，非所以持久也。

translations.literal: When calamities accumulate without resolution, when fighting ceases only to resume, when those nearby are miserable and those afar are terrified—this is not the way to endure.
translations.idiomatic: When calamities pile up without resolution, when fighting ceases only to resume, when those nearby despair and those far away tremble—this is no way to sustain an empire.

sentences.zh: 今天下鍛甲砥劍，橋箭累弦，轉輸運糧，未見休時，此天下之所共憂也。

translations.literal: Now the whole empire is forging armor, sharpening swords, straightening arrows, stringing bows, and transporting grain—with no end in sight. This is the shared worry of all under Heaven.
translations.idiomatic: Now across the entire empire, armor is being forged, swords sharpened, arrows straightened, bows strung, and grain transported—with no end in sight. This is the shared anxiety of all the people under Heaven.

sentences.zh: 夫兵久而變起，事煩而慮生。

translations.literal: When military campaigns drag on, upheaval arises; when affairs become burdensome, anxiety breeds.
translations.idiomatic: When war drags on, upheaval follows; when burdens multiply, discontent breeds.

sentences.zh: 今外郡之地或幾千里，列城數十，形束壤制，旁脅諸侯，非公室之利也。

translations.literal: Now the outer commanderies stretch for nearly a thousand li with dozens of walled cities. Their terrain constrains and controls the land, flanking and pressuring the feudal lords—this does not serve the interests of the imperial house.
translations.idiomatic: Now the outer commanderies stretch for nearly a thousand li, with dozens of walled cities arrayed along them. Their terrain constrains the surrounding land and presses upon the feudal lords—this does not serve the interests of the imperial house.

sentences.zh: 上觀齊晉之所以亡者，公室卑削，六卿大盛也；

translations.literal: Looking above at the reasons Qi and Jin fell: the ducal house was weakened and diminished while the Six Ministers grew overly powerful;
translations.idiomatic: Looking at the reasons Qi and Jin fell: the ducal house was weakened while the Six Ministers grew overly powerful;

sentences.zh: 下觀秦之所以滅者，嚴法刻深，欲大無窮也。

translations.literal: looking below at the reason Qin was destroyed: its laws were harsh and punishments cruel, and its ambitions were boundless.
translations.idiomatic: looking at the reason Qin was destroyed: its laws were harsh, its punishments cruel, and its ambitions knew no bounds.

sentences.zh: 今郡守之權，非特六卿之重也；

translations.literal: Now the power of the commandery governors is no less weighty than that of the Six Ministers of old;
translations.idiomatic: Now the power held by the commandery governors is no less than that once held by the Six Ministers;

sentences.zh: 地幾千里，非特閭巷之資也；

translations.literal: their territories stretch for nearly a thousand li—far more than the resources of an alleyway;
translations.idiomatic: their territories stretch nearly a thousand li—far beyond the resources of a mere alleyway;

sentences.zh: 甲兵器械，非特棘矜之用也：以遭萬世之變，則不可稱諱也。

translations.literal: their armor, weapons, and equipment are far more than thorny staffs. Should they encounter the upheavals that come once in ten thousand generations, the consequences would be unspeakable.
translations.idiomatic: and their armor, weapons, and equipment are far more formidable than thorny staffs. Should they encounter the kind of upheaval that comes once in ten thousand generations, the consequences would be too terrible to speak of.

sentences.zh: 書奏天子，天子召見三人，謂曰：「公等皆安在？

translations.literal: When the memorials were submitted to the Emperor, the Emperor summoned all three men and said: 'Where have you all been?
translations.idiomatic: When these memorials reached the Emperor, he summoned all three men and exclaimed: 'Where have you all been hiding?

sentences.zh: 何相見之晚也！」

translations.literal: Why have we met so late!'
translations.idiomatic: Why has it taken so long for us to meet!'

sentences.zh: 於是上乃拜主父偃、徐樂、嚴安為郎中。

translations.literal: Thereupon the Emperor appointed Zhufu Yan, Xu Yue, and Yan An as Gentlemen of the Palace.
translations.idiomatic: The Emperor then appointed Zhufu Yan, Xu Yue, and Yan An as Gentlemen of the Palace.

sentences.zh: 偃數見，上疏言事，詔拜偃為謁者，遷為中大夫。

translations.literal: Zhufu Yan was received in audience several times and submitted memorials on affairs of state. By imperial decree he was appointed Receptionist, then promoted to Palace Grandee.
translations.idiomatic: Zhufu Yan was received in audience several times and submitted memorials on affairs of state. By imperial decree, he was first appointed Receptionist and then promoted to Palace Grandee.

sentences.zh: 一歲中四遷偃。

translations.literal: Within a single year, Zhufu Yan was promoted four times.
translations.idiomatic: In the span of a single year, Zhufu Yan was promoted four times.

sentences.zh: 偃說上曰：「古者諸侯不過百里，彊弱之形易制。

translations.literal: Zhufu Yan advised the Emperor: 'In ancient times, feudal lords held no more than a hundred li. The balance of power between strong and weak was easy to maintain.
translations.idiomatic: Zhufu Yan advised the Emperor: 'In ancient times, a feudal lord's domain extended no more than a hundred li, and the balance of power between the strong and the weak was easy to control.

sentences.zh: 今諸侯或連城數十，地方千里，緩則驕奢易為淫亂，急則阻其彊而合從以逆京師。
